content.problemStatement:
Chef wants to find all unique combinations of exactly combinationCountcombinationCountcombinationCount numbers that add up to a target sum targetSumtargetSumtargetSum. 

Chef can only use numbers from 111 through 999, and each number can be used at most once in each combination. Chef wants to list all such valid combinations without any duplicates. 

Help Chef by writing a function to generate all these unique combinations.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindCombinationsfindCombinationsfindCombinations - This function finds all unique combinations of combinationCountcombinationCountcombinationCount numbers that add up to targetSumtargetSumtargetSum.

Parameters
combinationCountcombinationCountcombinationCount: The number of elements in each combination.
targetSumtargetSumtargetSum: The target sum that each combination must add up to.
Return Value
Returns an array of array containing all valid combinations.
Each inner array represents one combination of integers.
Combinations are unique and sorted in ascending order within each combination.
Constraints
2≤combinationCount≤92 \leq combinationCount \leq 92≤combinationCount≤9
1≤targetSum≤601 \leq targetSum \leq 601≤targetSum≤60

Input Format

The first line contains a single integerType TTT — the number of test cases.
Each test case contains two integers:

combinationCountcombinationCountcombinationCount — the number of elements in each combination.
targetSumtargetSumtargetSum — the target sum for the combinations.




Output Format

For each test case, print all unique combinations that satisfy the conditions.
Each combination should be printed as space-separated integers in ascending order on a separate line.
If no valid combinations exist, print an empty line.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two integers: combinationCount and targetSum.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print all unique combinations that satisfy the conditions. Each combination should be printed as space-separated integers in ascending order on a separate line. If no valid combinations exist, print an empty line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the combinations that sum to 15 are [1, 5, 9], [2, 4, 9], [2, 5, 8], [3, 4, 8], [3, 5, 7], and [4, 5, 6]. For the second test case, the combination that sums to 24 is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]. For the third test case, the combination that sums to 15 is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
For the first test case, the combination that sums to 17 is [8, 9]. For the second test case, the combinations that sum to 12 are [1, 2, 9], [1, 3, 8], [1, 4, 7], [2, 3, 7], [2, 4, 6], and [3, 4, 5].
There are no combinations that sum to 30.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a backtracking approach. We start by initializing an empty combination and then recursively add numbers to the combination, ensuring that the sum of the numbers does not exceed the target sum. If the sum exceeds the target sum, we backtrack and try a different combination.
solution.timeComplexity: O(9^combinationCount) per test case, O(T * 9^combinationCount) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(combinationCount) — the maximum depth of the recursion call stack.
